title: Masking during Training vs. Inference
concept: During training, masking lets us train on all target words in parallel; at inference, the decoder naturally only sees past words, so masking is built-in.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use masks in sequence models?

options.text: To prevent the model from seeing future words.
To make the text look prettier.

exercises.explain: Masking blocks the model from looking at the words that come next, ensuring it learns to predict honestly.

cards.title: The Secret of AI Speed
cards.body: Ever wondered why AI models train so fast but generate text one word at a time? It all comes down to how we hide the future during training versus how they work in the real world!

cards.title: Training vs. Inference Masking
cards.body: During training, masking hides future words so the model can learn to predict them all at once. During inference (generating new text), the model naturally predicts one word at a time, meaning the 'future' words don't even exist yet, so no artificial mask is needed!

cards.title: The Sticky Note Trick
cards.body: Imagine a student taking a practice test where some answers are covered with sticky notes (training mask) so they don't cheat. But on the real exam (inference), the questions are revealed one by one. The student naturally can't see the future questions because they haven't been written on the board yet!

cards.title: Translating in Action
cards.body: When training a translator on 'I love coding', we mask 'coding' while the model predicts 'love' in parallel. But when you use the translator in real life (inference) and it has only generated 'I love', the word 'coding' doesn't exist on the screen yet. The future is naturally hidden!

cards.title: The Masking Myth
cards.body: You might think we always need to run a masking algorithm to block out future words. In reality, during inference, there is nothing to block! The model builds the sentence step-by-step, so the future is empty.

cards.title: Two Different Worlds
cards.body: See how the presence of the future text changes between training and real-world use.

left.text: The whole book is open, so we must block the next pages.

right.text: You are writing the book page by page; future pages don't exist.

exercises.prompt: When do we actively use a masking matrix to hide future words?

options.text: During training, to stop the model from looking ahead at the complete target text.
During inference, because the model already knows the whole story.
Only when the model makes a mistake.
Only when we want to slow down the model.

exercises.explain: During training, we have the complete target sentence, so we must artificially mask the future words to prevent the model from looking ahead.

exercises.prompt: True or False: During inference, the decoder naturally only sees past words without needing a training mask.
exercises.explain: True! At inference time, the model generates text one word at a time, so future words do not exist to be seen.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word about why we mask during training.
exercises.sentence: Training masking allows the computer to process all words in _____ instead of one by one.
exercises.explain: Masking allows the model to calculate predictions for all positions at the same time (in parallel) during training.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements is generated by an incorrect AI bot?

options.text: During training, we mask the future because we feed the entire sentence into the model at once.
During inference, we must mask the future words to prevent the model from reading the user's mind.
During inference, the model naturally cannot see the future because those words have not been generated yet.

exercises.explain: The second option is AI-generated and incorrect because there are no future words to mask during generation. The model produces them one by one.

exercises.prompt: Why do we skip applying an active training mask during inference (generation)?

options.text: We need a stronger mask during inference to prevent cheating.
We do not need to mask because the future words do not exist yet.
The mask is only needed to speed up the hardware during inference.
We must mask the past words instead of the future words.

exercises.explain: At inference time, the model predicts one word at a time, generating its own input sequence. There is no future text to hide, so we do not need to apply a training mask matrix.
